Now, code in C++:

class Solution {
public:
    vector<vector<int>> largeGroupPositions(string s) {
        vector<vector<int>> result;
        if (s.empty()) return result;
        int start = 0;
        for (int i = 1; i < s.length(); i++) {
            if (s[i] != s[i-1]) {
                if (i-1 - start +1 >=3) {
                    result.push_back({start, i-1});
                }
                start = i;
            }
        }
        // process the last group
        int end = s.length() -1;
        if (end - start +1 >=3) {
            result.push_back({start, end});
        }
        return result;
    }
}

solution: 
```cpp
class Solution {
public:
    vector<vector<int>> largeGroupPositions(string s) {
        vector<vector<int>> result;
        if (s.empty()) return result;
        int start = 0;
        for (int i = 1; i < s.length(); i++) {
            if (s[i] != s[i-1]) {
                if (i - 1 - start + 1 >= 3) {
                    result.push_back({start, i - 1});
                }
                start = i;
            }
        }
        int end = s.length() - 1;
        if (end - start + 1 >= 3) {
            result.push_back({start, end});
        }
        return result;
    }
};
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(n)
space complexity: O(1)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution iterates through the string once, tracking the start of each group. When a group ends, it checks if the length is large (≥3) and records the interval. The final group is checked after the loop. This approach ensures linear time and constant auxiliary space. Key strengths include simplicity and optimal efficiency; weaknesses are none significant for the problem constraints.
